1:1.text: Year of the World 3668, Year before Christ 336.
Thus many Hebrew books commence, as Exodus, Josue, Baruch, etc.
Greece, over the whole. He was chosen generalissimo.
He conquered many of the Greeks, and overthrew Darius Condomanus, the year of the world 3674.
His dominion in Syria may be meant.
Alexander [the Great] was the first who reigned in that great monarchy, which he founded.

1:3.text: Earth, even to India, beyond which the ancients knew of nothing.
Quiet, or silent; depopulated, (Isaias 14:7.) and obedient, Habacuc 2:20.

1:4.text: Up. He was spoiled by prosperity, (Calmet) and would pass for a god, and be styled "king of the world."

1:6.text: Bed. Some suppose through poison, (Diodorus xvii.; St. Jerome, ad Laetam.) or excess of wine.
So perished the man who would claim divine honours!

1:7.text: Servants. On the ninth day of sickness which preceded his death, he admitted all his soldiers to kiss his hand. Then he asked his friends, if they thought they should find such another king? As they were silent, he predicted what bloodshed would ensue, and told them to bury him in the temple of Ammon. When they asked to whom he left the throne, he said, "to the most worthy." He gave his ring to Perdiccas; whence all inferred, that he should be regent till the proper heirs came of age. Perdiccas desired to know when he would have divine honours: to which Alexander [the Great] replied with these his last words, "when you are happy:" (Just. xii.; Diodorus xvii.) as much as to say, never.
This seems contrary to the sacred historian. But his authority is far superior.

Kingdom. This is otherwise by Q. Curtius; though he acknowledges that divers were of that opinion, and that it had been delivered by some authors. (liber[book] x.) But here we find from the sacred text that he was in error.
By delivering his ring to Perdiccas, Alexander gave him authority to make this division.
Many assert, that he gave him directions in his last will. This was observed for some time, ver. 10.

1:8.text: Year of the World 3681, Year before Christ 323.
Twelve. Josephus, etc. Some add, a few months. He was about thirty-three years old.

1:10.text: Death, twelve years being expired, when all the posterity of Alexander [the Great] were dead. His generals gradually destroyed each other, and four took the diadem at the same time: Ptolemy, in Egypt; Seleucus, in Syria; Cassander, in Macedonia; and Lysimachus, in Thrace.
Those who choose to read in order, may, after the preface [in] 2 Machabees 2:20., read the above, then 2 Machabees 3:1.

1:11.text: Year of the World 3829, Year before Christ 175.
Antiochus the illustrious; Epiphanes, the younger son of Antiochus the great, who usurped the kingdom, to the prejudice of his nephew Demetrius, son of his elder brother, Seleucus Philopater.
He took this title, particularly after the Samaritans had offered it to him.
His medals have always God prefixed, "the god appearing;" as he came opportunely, like a god, to defend the kingdom against the Egyptians, after he had been fourteen years a hostage at Rome.

1:11.src: St. Jerome, in Daniel viii., and ix.; Atheneus 5:4., and 10:12.
1:11.text: Nothing could be more abject than his character.
Greeks, counting not from the beginning of the reign of Alexander [the Great], but from the first year of Seleucus Nicator.
The era of the Seleucides began in spring or autumn, the year of the world 3692, the year before Christ 312, though the Chaldeans and Ptolemy date from the following spring.
The Grecian monarchy had then stood twenty years.

1:12.text: Men. Jason attempted to supplant Onias III and introduced pagan customs, when he was dead. Menelaus treated him in like manner. Being cited to pay what he had promised, he left Lysimachus in his place, who was slain in the temple, which he was robbing, 2 Machabees 4:--- Us. Thus the Jews complained in the days of Jeremias, (xliv. 18.) and the Christian religion was blamed for all the miseries which fell upon the Roman empire.
St. Augustine proves in his City of God, that this accusation was false.

1:16.src: St. Epiphanius; Pond. xvi.; Bartol.; 1 Corinthians 7:18.
1:16.text: Made. St. Jerome thinks the attempt useless. But the Jews discovered the art.
Sold to the devil, becoming his instruments, 3 Kings 21:25., and Romans 7:14.

1:17.text: Year of the World 3834, Year before Christ 170.
Egypt. He coveted this kingdom, having Syria already.

He was not very fit for war: but Egypt was not under Philometor a child, and Ephphanes made an easy conquest. This was his second visit to that country. While he besieged Alexandria, a report of his death was spread; and as he was informed that the Jews rejoiced at it, he fell upon them, ver. 21.

1:23.text: Sanctuary. Josephus (Antiquities 12:6.) writes, that Antiochus slew such as opposed his entrance into the city, and afterwards those who opened the gates, but would have prevented his plundering the temple.
Menelaus conducted the king, 2 Machabees 5:15. Profane authors condemn this conduct of the king towards a city subject to him.
Diodorus relates several fables on this occasion.

1:30.text: Year of the World 3836, Year before Christ 168.
He basely fell upon the unsuspecting citizens on a sabbath day.

1:33.text: Fire, thus destroying a great part.

1:35.text: The city of David. That is, the castle of Sion.
In it was placed a garrison of Macedonians.
This castle had been occupied by Syrians before. It was now enlarged, 2 Machabees 4:27., and 5:5.

1:38.text: Sanctuary, to pillage all who came to the temple.
An evil devil. Literally, an adversary, watching constantly to do harm, as the evil spirit is always watching and seeking whom he could devour.

1:40.text: Fled with Judas, etc. (2 Machabees v.) Josephus (Antiquities 12:7.) assures us that the Samaritans flattered Epiphanes, and begged their temple might be consecrated to Jupiter, etc. They pretended that they were of Sidonian extraction.

1:43.text: Year of the World 3837, Year before Christ 167.
Be one. This reason herself teaches. But the king wished to establish a false religion, or rather he desired to root out all religion, and to shew his authority.
Demere superstitionem et mores Graecorum dare adnixus.
He was not content with plunder, unless he ruined souls.

1:45.text: Service, or worship, (Calmet) introduced by Epiphanes.

1:49.text: People. He forced them to eat forbidden meat.

1:57.text: Fifteenth. In all other places the 25th, whence this may be incorrect, (Calmet) or the altar was now set up, (Haydock) though sacrifices were not yet offered.
Idol. The statue of Jupiter Olympius, (Challoner) as Daniel (ix.) had foretold, as a figure of what antichrist would do, (Matthew xxiv.; Worthington) as well as the Romans, when they took Jerusalem.

1:59.text: Law, and all the sacred writings.
The pious Jews secreted what they could, and Judas took care to collect them, 2 Machabees 1:14., and 3:48.

1:61.text: After month. The officers took an account of the slain, (Grotius) or the dedication of the statue was renewed, or rather the king's nativity was kept, 2 Machabees 6:7.
But this would only occur once a-year. People were compelled to sacrifice on the same day of each month on which the statue had been dedicated, ver. 62.

1:62.text: Over-against, or (Haydock) "upon," epi. The Greek altars were of a smaller size.

1:65.text: Many. Eleazar, and the seven Machabees, etc.

1:67.text: Wrath. God seemed displeased, as he was with too many. The pious were exposed to various trials, and to the fury of persecutors.

2:1.text: Joarib. This family was the most distinguished, as Josephus, who sprung from it, asserts, (Haydock) being the first of the twenty-four families of priests, 1 Paralipomenon 24:7, 19.
Mathathias was probably a descendant of Phinees, (ver. 54.) and was officiating when the impious orders arrived. Whereupon he retired to Modin, (Calmet) near Diospolis.
Many say he was chosen high priest; and therefore decided the question about fighting on the sabbath. But any other, in similar emergencies, might have given that decision.
Judas was chosen, (Josephus) and Jonathas exercised the functions.

2:1.src: St. Jerome; St. Augustine, contra Faust. 1:72.
2:1.text: To verify the prediction of Jacob, some have concluded that the Machabees were allied to the tribe of Juda by the mother's side.
Yet this is not proved, nor requisite. They were called Asmoneans, either from the town of Asamon, (Josue 15:27.) or from Assamoneus, great grandfather of Mathathias.
This family ruled till Herod, 128 years.
Here an account is given of their wars, and more fully in the eight last chapters of the second Book[2 Machabees].
Perhaps John, Mathathias, etc., were chosen high priests by the people, while the king sent others.

2:2.text: Gaddis, "a troop, fortunate, or a heap of wheat."

2:3.text: Thasi, Syriac, "ardent," or feeble, (Calmet) or a destroyer.

2:4.text: Machabeus. The common etymology has been given [in the Introduction].

But Judas had then no banners, and there is no proof that the Jews used such abbreviations, (Calmet) though moderns write Rambam, for "R. Moses, son of Maimon," Moce-bie, (Haydock) "conquering in God," (equivalent to Nicator, the title of Sedeucus) seems better, particularly as the word is written Machabee.
Yet this is liable to the objection of c being placed instead of k, which occurs invariably in the Chaldean and Syriac.

2:5.text: Apphus, "the wrath or flying of counsel," (Pagnin) "fainting, abundant, or dissipating."
This small help, of great importance, was foretold by Daniel, 11:34.

2:7.text: Ruin. St. Polycarp used a similar expression.

2:14.text: Lamentation. By voluntary mortification, he sought to appease God's indignation.

2:18.text: They. Josephus mentions Apelles. Persecutors have thus frequently attempted to delude the faithful; first by flattery, and then by torments.

2:21.text: Profitable. But quite the reverse both for this world and for the next. It is a dreadful crime.

2:22.text: Way. Greek, "to the right or to the left."

2:24.text: Law. Deuteronomy 11:6., (Menochius) and 13:9. Those who attempted to introduce the worship of idols, were to be slain without farther trial.
Mathathias was then judge and high priest.

2:24.src: St. Cyprian, exhort. 5.; St. Bernard, ep. 158.; Worthington
2:24.text: He acted not though private revenge.

2:29.text: And justice. The ceremonial and moral law, (Calmet) or to live piously.

2:34.text: Sabbath. By sacrificing to idols, or by coming forth to fight.
They were of opinion that the latter was unlawful, and their ignorance might be excused.

2:37.text: Innocency. Literally, "simplicity," has often this sense. Their piety was not, however, enlightened, as the ceremonial law gives way on such occasions.
Their simplicity is commended by St. Ambrose, Of. 1:40. Yet they had not the prudence of serpents, which is also requisite. The Church remains.

2:41.text: Come up. They say not that they will attack.
Pompey perceived this, and made his advances and works on the sabbath, without opposition.
Pompey I had thus taken Jerusalem.
The Samaritans will not move on that day.

The Jews now take care of the sick, but will not help a beast out of the ditch on the sabbath, though in our Saviour's time they did just the reverse, Luke 14:5. The law has thus been variously explained. The Machabees judged with prudence. Yet superstition had again gained ground, when Josephus (vita) would not fight or assemble his forces on the sabbath, as being contrary to the law. In a storm, a Jewish pilot abandoned the helm till the danger was evident.

2:42.text: The Assideans. A set of men that led a religious life; and were zealous for the law and worship of God.
They fought like the knights of Malta.
Esidim (Haydock) means "the pious," Ecclesiasticus 44:10. Many think they were the same with the Cinites, Rechabites, and Essenes. From them sprung the Pharisees.
The society was intent on adorning the temple, (Scaliger) by which they swore, Matthew 23:6.
They were like monks in the old law.
These Essenes followed piety, avoiding the hypocrisy and error of the Pharisees and Sadducees.

2:48.text: Horn. That is, they suffered not the power of Antiochus, that man of sin, to abolish the law and religion of God.

2:53.text: Lord, next to the king, Genesis 41:40.

2:54.text: Everlasting, as long as the old law should subsist.

2:55.text: He and Caleb alone gave a true relation.

2:66.text: People. Order is peculiarly requisite in all things, so here the proper persons are chosen, to whom each must submit in their sphere.
Mathathias speaks as chief of the nation.
Judas was his eldest son, and Simon the fourth.

2:70.src: The year of the world 3838.; Calmet
2:70.text: Year. Not of his age, but of the Greeks, (Menochius) after being at the head about twelve months.

3:1.text: His skill and valour also entitled him to this post.
He did not claim this honour, but received it from his father, and was obeyed by the virtuous people.

3:7.text: Kings. Epiphanes, Eupator,and Demetrius, with their generals.
Judas gained four great battles against Apollonius, Seron, Gorgias, and Lysias, 1 Machabees 4:1, 28.

3:8.text: Wrath. Making the enemy retire, (Haydock) and purifying the temple, etc.

3:9.text: Perishing. So the Jews style the miserable.

3:10.text: Apollonius, who had pillaged Jerusalem, 2 Machabees 5:24.

3:16.text: Bethoron, seven leagues north of Jerusalem.

3:17.text: Fasting. Judas thus laudably prepared for battle.
Human frailty fears to engage a strong enemy. But true confidence in God gives courage and victory.

3:25.text: Them. Philip wrote from Jerusalem to Ptolemy, to send aid. He immediately despatched Nicanor and Gorgias, who doubted not of success, so that they brought the merchants to buy the Jews, 2 Machabees viii.

3:27.text: Year of the World 3839, Year before Christ 165.

3:29.text: Land. He formerly received three hundred talents from Judea; now he was forced to send money thither.
Times. He not only disturbed the Jews, but other nations, which rebelled.
Though he had plundered the temple, he was still poor: the usual fate of wicked princes.

Sacrilege seldom prospers. When Henry VIII took the revenues of monasteries, he was still needy. They had amply provided for the poor. Soon after, however, the poor rates commenced; and in the 39th Eliz.[Elizabeth?], no less than eleven bills were brought into Parliament for their relief. (Dugdale.) See Hist. of Prot. Ch.[Challoner?]

3:30.text: Liberal. Epiphanes left many vestiges of his magnificence.

The very year when Apollonius was defeated, (Usher, the year of the world 3838.; Calmet) he exhibited most costly games at Daphne, near Antioch, to excel those of P. Emilius, at Amphipolis. Yet he disgusted the company with his apeish conduct.

3:31.text: Persia. The Arabic (2 Machabees vii.) says the king had revolted.

3:37.text: Countries, and took Artaxias, king of Armenia.

3:38.text: Ptolomee. He had been governor of Cyprus, under Philometor, and gave him the revenue only when he was capable of acting for himself.
He afterwards betrayed the island to Epiphanes, 2 Machabees viii.

3:40.text: Emmaus, two leagues west of Jerusalem, (Calmet) different from that mentioned in St. Luke.

3:41.text: Servants. Josephus (Antiquities 12:11.) adds "chains," which were all turned against them by Judas. Nicanor had promised ninety Jews for a talent, 2 Machabees 8:10.

3:44.text: Pray. This, with fasting and other penitential works, is the best armour in holy wars of religion.

3:46.text: Maspha. Silo had been, and Jerusalem was afterwards, the place for public prayer.
As the Jews could not go to Jerusalem, they performed their duty as well as they could at Maspha, where Israel had often met, Judges xx., and xxi., and 1 Kings 7:5., and 10:17.

3:48.text: In which. The Greek text seems corrupt. By a small change we might render, "while."
From some passages abused, the pagans sought to establish their religion.
For. Many Greek copies add, "to inscribe," (Menochius) or "draw upon them the likenesses of their idols."
Thus they wished to defile the books, as they had done the temple, (Menochius) and render them useless to the Jews.
Syriac, "law, before the sanctuary on account of the Gentiles, who wished to make them imitate their idolatry."
The sacred books would be a good preservative.

3:49.text: Ornaments, which they had rescued from plunder. It seems they had also set up a sort of tabernacle, and had some ornament from the sanctuary.
Days. They ought then to offer victims, which was now impossible.

3:54.text: Trumpets. Protection seemed to be promised, Numbers 10:9.

3:56.text: Yet his forces were already few.

3:60.text: Done. This godly resolution inspired them with courage, and procured the victory.

4:1.text: Year of the World 3839, Year before Christ 165.

4:2.text: Guides. Several were apostates. Judas was at or near Maspha.

4:3.text: Rose up. The vigilance of rulers and pastors preserves from the devil's stratagems.

4:4.text: Camp, foraging, as they despised the forces of Judas, and supposed he was in the camp, or engaged with Gorgias.

4:6.text: Three. We find seven thousand elsewhere. He was at the head of three thousand, and set his three brothers over fifteen hundred each.
Who. Some copies read, "because." He could arm no more completely.
Swords. This is added in Greek and in some Latin copies. Having already gained two victories, they must have had some swords, with which they slew the fugitives, ver. 15.
They were ill armed, through poverty (Josephus) chiefly, (Haydock) using slings, Zacharias 9:15.
Confidence in God procureth his assistance, ver. 8.

4:13.text: Trumpet. There were many priests in the army.

4:15.text: Idumea. Alexandrian manuscript reads "Judea," which seems more probable. The enemy fled on all sides, some among the Philistines; (Calmet) others to the southern parts of Juda, occupied by Edom.

4:23.text: Sea. This sort of purple was most esteemed. The art of dying it is now lost. The merchants' goods fell into the hands of the Jews. The pursuit was interrupted by the sabbath. At various times Bacchides and Timothy lost above 20,000; Phylarchus and Callisthenes perished; and Nicanor saved himself by flight. The booty was laid up in places of security, 2 Machabees 8:25, 30.

4:24.text: For ever. This was the chorus of Psalm cxxxv., which was sung; (Calmet) or the [Psalm] cxvii. might be used on this occasion.

4:27.text: Commanded. See what followed, 2 Machabees 8:8., to the end.

4:32.text: Destruction. Let them perish, or fall upon each other.

4:38.text: Burnt by Callisthenes, whom the Jews burnt in a house, 2 Machabees viii.
Chambers; (pastophoria. Septuagint) places for beds, veils, etc.

4:38.src: Rufin, Hist. 2:23.; Com. Apost.[Apostolic Constitutions?] 2:57; Clement [of Alexandria,] paed. 3:2.; Calmet
4:38.text: St. Jerome commonly prefers "the treasury," with Aquila, or "beds," as Symmachus translates. Chambers for the officers of the temple were common both among pagans and Christians.
As it was the chief design of Judas to defend religion, so he presently purifies the temple.

4:41.text: Fight, or to be ready, if the garrison should offer to molest them (Calmet) from the higher part of Sion, while they were performing what religion required of them.

4:43.text: Defiled. Altars, temples, and statues of false gods made of stone, and set up in the temple, (chap. 1:50.; Worthington) and before the houses, (2 Machabees 10:1.) were now demolished and thrown into the vale of Cedron, 2 Paralipomenon 29:16.

4:44.text: Profaned with impure victims, and with the altar of Jupiter erected above it, 1 Machabees 1:23.

4:46.text: Stones; rough, and covered with brass: (Exodus 20:25.) though this latter circumstances is not certain with respect to the altar built by the captives.
Prophet. None was recognized after Malachias, as God prepared his people to pay more attention to the Messias.

4:47.text: Law. Nothing was specified. The altar in the desert was of wood. At Sinai and Hebal, turf or rough stones were used; whence unhewed stones were judged most proper.

4:48.text: Up, or repaired the temple chambers, etc.

4:51.text: Veils, at the entrance of the holy of holies.

4:52.src: The year [of the kingdom of the Greeks?] 145.; Worthington
4:52.text: Year. The temple was purified a little more than two years after it had been profaned.

4:52.src: St. Thomas Aquinas or some other com.[commentator]; Usher, the year of the world 3840.; Tirinus; Calmet; Salien; Menochius
4:52.text: The sacrifices had been interrupted just three years. The Second Book [of Machabees] 10:3. specifies two years; whence some infer, that the temple was twice dedicated. But these two years most probably refer to the administration of Judas.

4:57.text: Escutcheons, or bucklers of gold, taken from the soldiers of Antiochus, 1 Machabees 6:39. Such ornaments were placed in temples, to testify the gratitude of the people. They wished to restore as much as possible what had been taken away, 1 Machabees 1:23. On this occasion the Jews carried branches in memory of what they had suffered in desert places for three years. Hence they style it the feast of tabernacles of Casleu, 2 Machabees 1:9., and 10:6.

4:59.text: Decreed. Our Saviour observed this [Hanukkah] festival, which was appointed so long after Moses, John 10:22.
It was styled Encoenia, and kept in winter, while Solomon's temple was dedicated in Tisri, and Zorobabel's in Adar. The Jews light lamps, (Calmet) to testify their joy.

The Rabbins add, that a small bottle of oil had been preserved by the high priest, which multiplied so as to supply the lamps during the whole octave. Hence they still have lights at their windows on this festival.

4:61.text: Bethsura, which might receive reinforcements from Sion; or rather, (Calmet) Greek, they "fortified Bethsura to keep it, (Sion) that," etc.
Idumea. The people of that country had seized many cities of Juda.

5:1.text: Nations. They were always ready to oppress the Jews. Thus God punished or tried his people.
Judas, or his brethren, here gains eight victories over them.

5:3.text: Idumea. Alexandrian manuscript has, "Judea." Both may be right. The country had been wrested from Juda by the Idumeans.
Acrabathane is "the ascent of scorpions," (hakrabim) which were probably numerous, to the south of the Dead Sea, Numbers 34:4., and Deuteronomy 8:15.

5:4.text: Bean; perhaps Beon, near the sea of Sodom, (Calmet) or Bathaner, beyond the Jordan [River].

5:5.text: Destruction. Sometimes the people were spared. All depended on the wording of the vow, Leviticus 27:28., and Numbers 21:1. See other particulars, 2 Machabees 10:16., to 23.

5:8.text: Year of the World 3481, Year before Christ 163.
Gazer, in Ephraim. But Jazer, in the tribe of Gad, seems to be meant, as the Greek expresses it.
Afterwards Judas returned and defeated Timotheus, 2 Machabees 10:24.

5:9.text: Galaad. Most of the towns had been seized by the Ammonites, etc. After the return of the captives, they were probably driven out. They attempted to regain possession, when Epiphanes declared against the Jews.
Datheman; Petra, (Calmet) or perhaps Rathma, Numbers 33:18.

5:11.text: Timotheus. Another of this name had been slain the preceding year, [the year of the world] 3840, at Gazara, 2 Machabees 10:37.

5:13.text: Tubin, or Tob, to the south of Gad, Judges 11:3.

5:15.text: Is filled. Greek Alexandrian, "All Galilee of the Gentiles to destroy us."
This part of Galilee was occupied by pagans, who were excited by Epiphanes to fall upon the Jews, 2 Machabees 6:8.

5:23.text: Arbatis. Arboth means "deserts" uncultivated, here probably denoting the valley of Jezrahel. Simon wisely concentrated the people, as Judas did in Galaad, ver. 45.

5:24.text: Desert; probably from Bethsan to Bosor, marching with all secrecy.

5:25.text: Nabutheans; descendants of Ismael's eldest son, whose capital was Petra. They continued friends to the Jews.

5:26.text: Barasa; probably the Bozra of Moab. Greek, "Bosorra."
Bosor means a fortress. It is not wonderful that there should be many places of this name in Arabia, to defend the people from robbers.
Casphor, or rather Casbon, (ver. 36.) the famous Hesebon, Numbers 21:26.
Mageth, or Machati; (Grotius) though it seems too far off, Deuteronomy 3:14.
Carnaim, "horns," being sacred to the moon, (Calmet) called Astaroth, Genesis 14:5.

5:36.text: Bosor, or Bosra, ver. 26. Bosor was taken before, ver. 28.

5:37.text: After. Judas had in the mean time defeated Lysias, 2 Machabees 11:1., etc.
Raphon, or Raphana; (Grotius) or as that was too remote, probably Saphon, Numbers 32:35.

5:41.text: Afraid. He interpreted the delay in this sense, which was very precarious, (Haydock) as nothing could be more superstitious. Jonathan begged for a similar proof of God's favour. But Timotheus consulted only himself, 1 Kings 14:9.

5:42.text: Scribes; tribunes, who kept a register of the soldiers.
They were officers of great power.

5:44.text: Temple of Atergata, or Astarte. Judas slew 55,000. Timotheus was spared, 2 Machabees 12:20, 26.

5:46.text: Ephron, beyond the Jordan [River]. The situation is unknown. With regard to the right which Judas had to treat it with such severity, see Numbers 20:18., and Judges 8:5.
It probably belonged to God's people, and was unjustly detained.

5:53.text: A good general takes care of the weakest, and exhorts all to behave valiantly.

5:54.text: Not one. The 12,000 who defeated the Madianites, were preserved by God in like manner, Numbers 31:49.

5:55.text: Year of the World 3841, Year before Christ 163.

5:56.text: And Azarias. These two are known only for their disobedience and misconduct. In holy wars, God does not favour those who are actuated by base motives, ver. 18, 60.

Men who depend on themselves, and who are not properly authorized, receive not assistance from above; so these failed, as they were not chosen like those who were to save Israel, (Worthington) of the family of the Machabees.

5:58.text: Jamnia, a maritime city of the Philistines near Joppe. Gorgias, an experienced warrior, had retired thither, 1 Machabees 4:5., and 2 Machabees 8:9.

5:62.text: Israel. The late Rev. Robert Bannister applied this to Protestant writers of sermons, to dissuade young divines from applying to such authors as Tillotson, etc. It may also shew the reason why heretics succeed so ill in their foreign missions.
They know how to pervert Catholics, not how to convert infidels.

5:63.text: Men. Greek, "man, Judas, and his brethren were," etc.
See a similar expression [in] Exodus 14:3., respecting Moses.

5:65.text: Chebron, or Hebron, which the Idumeans had seized. Judas retook it after he had defeated Gorgias, 2 Machabees 12:32.

5:66.text: Samaria. This seems too remote. Some substitute Saraia; (Jos.[Josue?] 15:35.) others Maresa, (2 Machabees 12:35.) Marissa, (Jos.[Josephus?]) the same as Ressa, Numbers 33:21.

5:67.text: Desiring. They were only too brave. This quality was not incompatible with the priesthood. Greek Alexandrian has, "while he (Judas) was desiring," etc. Others have, "priests of the cities, while they," etc. The Roman edition agrees with us, (Calmet) and Grabe prints accordingly.

6:1.text: Year of the World 3840, Year before Christ 164.
The city. Greek Alexandrian, etc., "a city in Elymais," etc. The Roman copy, Syriac, Josephus, style the place Elymais, where the temple was.
Profane authors agree that this temple was very rich, and that Epiphanes attempted to plunder it.
Nanea. Venus or Diana was the deity there adored, whom the king pretended he would marry, 2 Machabees 1:13., and 9:2.
The account of the death of Epiphanes is given to ver. 16., and 2 Machabees ix.

6:4.text: Battle. It is not known whether Elymais belonged to the king. Strabo (16) observes it was very jealous of its liberty, which it maintained against the Persians and the successors of Alexander [the Great].

Babylonia. At last he returned towards the country of Babylon. But before he arrived, the news of his generals' bad success in Judea filled him with vexation, and brought on desperate diseases, ver. 8., and 2 Machabees 9:5.
He intended to pass by the country or city of Babylon, but was prevented by illness on the mountains which divide it from Persia.

6:5.text: Persia, at Ecbatana. He expired at Tabis, having fallen from his chariot, etc., 2 Machabees ix.

6:12.text: Evils. All this repentance was fictitious, 2 Machabees ix.

6:14.text: Friends, educated with him. He appointed him regent instead of Lysias.
His son Eupator was only nine years old.
He made him appear and be recognized by the army. Eupator means one "born of a good father."

6:16.text: Nine. He began his persecution the year [of the kingdom of the Greeks?] 143, so that it lasted six years and almost four months, or 2300 days, (Daniel 8:14.) during which time Judas purified the temple, some months before the death of Epiphanes.

6:20.text: Fifty. How then was peace made in [the year of the kingdom of the Greeks?] 148, as we read [in] 2 Machabees 11:21.? Usher ([the year of the world] 3841) supposes the era was reckoned according to the Chaldean custom, which defers it six months. Basnage (II. 1.) rather thins that the letter is placed out of its proper order, and should occur at the beginning of Eupator's reign. See 2 Machabees 10.
Slings, or to thrown stones, etc., ballistas.
The Greek and Josephus rather indicate terraces, or towers where the machines were placed.

6:30.text: Hundred. The 2 Machabees 11:2., specifies 80,000. But it speaks of a subsequent action.

6:31.text: Idumea. The passes on the north were probably occupied.
Bethsura lay to the south of Jerusalem.

6:32.text: Bethzacharam, a defile (Josephus) between the city and Bethsura. Judas abandoned the siege of the castle on Sion.

6:34.text: Blood of grapes, or wine, (Deuteronomy 32:14.) and the juice of mulberries incite elephants to fight, as the smell of some sorts of blood causes dogs to hunt.
This might be done to accustom the elephants to the sight of blood.
White colours irritate them most, as red do bulls.
Sometimes wine and spirits were given them to drink, when they were to trample on criminals: but it was only shewn them when going to battle, as by drinking they lose their strength.

6:37.text: Thirty-two. Bochart looks upon this and other such accounts as fabulous. He does not determine how many might fight in these towers; but allows that there must have been above two or three, as some would correct the Greek text, "two or three strong men fighting with darts upon them." An elephant has been known to carry above 5000 pounds, and thirty-two men would weigh no more than 4800. Pliny ([Natural History?] 8:7.) observes that sixty people have been seen upon an elephant. Eupator probably had his from India, where they are larger than in Africa. A person of that country was deemed fittest to manage them.

6:38.text: Trumpets. It would seem as if the infantry had none. Yet this does not appear probable. The Greek reads in a different manner. Some copies have "to stir up and enclose them in the defiles," which seems useless; or "to keep them together in the phalanxes," (Alexandrian manuscript; Calmet) as the Vulgate has read.

6:39.text: Gold. The Argyraspides of Alexander [the Great] had "shields of silver." His successors might surpass this magnificence; or the officers might have golden and the soldiers brazen bucklers.

6:42.text: Six, etc., before the exploit of Eleazer. Others fell afterwards, 2 Machabees 11:11., specifies 11,000 foot, and 600 horse. Josephus says 1000 of the vanguard.

6:43.text: He is styled Abaron, (chap. 2:5.) the brother of Judas.

Arabic says "his servant." This feat is attributed to Judas, (2 Machabees 13:15.) as he was general, and approved of, or "commanded" Eleazar to act thus, according to Ben. Gorion, (III. 20.) and the Arabic.
Harness, for greater security. The skin is very hard, except under the belly.
Elephants in the army of Antiochus the great, or rather "the coward," were richly adorned.
Porus, king of India, rode on one covered with gold.

6:44.text: Name. This motive has made some condemn the exploit. But surely a person may seek to acquire fame, even by exposing himself to danger. Eleazar might well hope that the beast would not fall so suddenly. The other motive specified is truly noble and virtuous, and we cannot condemn this hero without stronger proofs. St. Gregory (Mor. 19:13.) represents him as a figure of the proud: but he might not therefore be one of the number, (Calmet) no more than Esau, who was a type of the reprobate, Malachias 1:3.
St. Ambrose (Off. 1:40.) highly commends the fortitude of this soldier exposing himself to the danger of death for religion.
This is the general sentiment. See Serarius; Menochius; Tirinus; and Grotius, jure. 3:4. a. 18.

6:46.text: Under it. The rhinoceros attacks the elephant in that most vulnerable part.

6:47.text: Them. Judas thought proper to retire to the temple, which alone was fortified, and in his power. The citadel of Sion held for the king, and the city was defenceless. Only what lay to the north of Bethsura was then called Judea. The Idumeans occupied the rest. Eupator blockaded the temple, and besieged Bethsura, which he took, ver. 41. This siege is related more at length, 2 Machabees 12:19. It cost the king a great deal.

6:51.text: Battering slings. Literally, balistas. (Haydock) See ver. 20.
Fire, or the long javelin, falarica, mentioned by Livy xxi. Incita sulcatum tremula secat aera flamma.
Pieces. Literally, "scorpions," (Haydock) a sort of pointed dart, (Calmet) filled with poison, virus qua figit effundit.

6:55.text: King. Lysias had thus been displaced, ver. 14.
Providence permitted that he should seek his own interest, and thus deliver Judas from the most imminent danger.

6:58.text: Come. Literally, "give our right hands," (Haydock) the sign of the most inviolable engagements among the Persians.

6:59.text: Before, by leave of Cyrus, Alex.[Alexander the Great], etc.
Despising. Greek, "abolished." They had made the attempt.

6:62.text: Oath. Eupator was not above ten years old, (ver. 14, 20.) so that Lysias must bear the greatest blame. He seems to have prepossessed the king against Philip, the regent.

7:1.text: Year of the World 3842, Year before Christ 162.

7:1.src: Polybius cxiv., and cxxii.; Josephus; Calmet
7:1.text: Rome. He had been a hostage instead of Epiphanes, who usurped his throne, 1 Machabees 1:11. After the death of the latter, the senate sent three legates to administer the kingdom, during the minority of Eupator. One of them was slain. The king and Lysias sent to exculpate themselves, but received no satisfactory answer. Demetrius thought this a proper opportunity for regaining the throne. He consulted the historian, Polybius, who advised him to depart privately. He, however, addressed the senate for leave, and being refused, fled with eight attendants to Tripoli, and thence to Apamea. His rivals durst not attack him, as he pretended to be sent by the Romans. The army of Eupator seized their king and Lysias, and put them to death.
Seleucus and Epiphanes were brothers; and of course Demetrius was cousin to Eupator, (Worthington) and the lawful heir of Seleucus.

7:5.text: Alcimus. He occupied the place of Menalaus, and would be high priest, but that office was held by the Machabees.

Eupator had slain Menalaus, as being too turbulent. Onias IV ought to have been recognized; (Calmet) but he fled into Egypt, and there built a temple. Lysias advised the king to select one from a family of less power. Alcimus was such, though of the posterity of Aaron, ver. 14.
He had fallen under Epiphanes, and the Jews would not receive him.

7:6.text: Thy friends, and the apostates, who obeyed the decree of the king's uncle. This was true, and the glory of Judas. See 2 Machabees 14:3.
They gained the king's favour by presents, and falsely accused Judas and the people.

7:8.text: River, as governor of Mesopotamia, (Josephus; Usher; Calmet) instead of Heraclides and Timarchus, who had abused their power. Hence Demetrius obtained the title of Soter, or "saviour."
Faithful. The chief man under the king.

7:12.text: Scribes. They were both civil and military officers.

7:13.text: Assideans. These were first consulted, as being most learned and pious, 1 Machabees 2:42.
Alcimus deceived them, and cruelly murdered 60, (Worthington) some scribes.

7:17.text: Them. The Psalm lxxviii. alludes to the Assideans, (ver. 2.) and is explained by many, as relating to this massacre.

7:19.text: Bethzecha. Greek, "Beseth," or Bethsetta, near the great plain. Hence Bacchides intended to return.

7:25.text: Crimes. He made the king some presents, of what he had probably stolen from the temple, and after waiting for some time in silence, began to accuse the people, 2 Machabees xiv.
See what is there related to ver. 12.

7:26.text: Enemy. He was one of the most terrible: yet Judas at last slew him, ver. 43., and 2 Machabees 15:28.
Nicanor was high in the king's favour, and had fled with him from Rome.

7:26.src: Usher, year of the world 3842.; Calmet
7:26.text: He first sent three ambassadors to Judas, in answer to what Simon had proposed; and peace was concluded. Alcimus hereupon accused Nicanor, and the king sent him an order to seize Judas. While he was endeavouring to comply, Judas escaped. He was again much exposed; (ver. 29.) and therefore collected his army, and fought near Jerusalem, 2 Machabees 14:15.

7:31.text: Capharsalama, "the field of peace," concluded perhaps with Eupator, (chap. 6:62.; Haydock) near Jerusalem.

7:32.text: Thousand. Some Greek copies have "hundred." Others think that these were Jews: but the contrary seems to be the case, though Judas was again (chap. 6:47.) forced to retreat to the temple, which he left shortly after, 2 Machabees 14:31.

7:33.text: King. The Jews observed the laudable custom of praying for their rulers, as Christians do, 1 Machabees 12:11., and Baruch 1:10., and 1 Timothy 2:1.
Sacrificamus pro salute Imperatoris sed Deo nostro et ipsius.

7:34.text: Abused. Literally, "polluted them," or grated their ears (Haydock) with his "blasphemies."
On such occasions, the Jews stopped their ears, Acts 7:56.

7:38.text: Longer. Read 2 Machabees 14:11. The last battle with Nicanor is described, 1 Machabees 15., and here, ver. 39.

7:40.text: Adarsa, in Ephraim, four miles from the lower Bethoron.

7:41.text: Sennacherib. Some copies omit this name.

7:45.text: Gazara, distant a day's journey from Adarsa, ver. 40., and 1 Machabees 5:8.
Signals, to admonish all. Greek, "with the trumpets for signals," used for that purpose by the priests.

7:46.text: Horns, like bulls, (Calmet) or they surrounded them with the wings of the army.

7:47.text: Jerusalem, and the temple, without the walls. His tongue was given to the birds, 2 Machabees 15:33.

7:49.text: Thirteenth. The day before the feast of Purim, (Haydock) or "lots." It was observed in the time of Josephus.

7:50.text: Time. While Judas settles religious matters, Demetrius prepares for war. Read 2 Machabees 15:1.

8:1.text: Romans. Many historians, Livy, Plutarch, etc., record the same.
They comply with their just requests.
The Scripture speaks of the fine times of the republic, and gives this temporal reward to the virtues of the Romans, which they so greatly sought, and to which alone they were entitled.

"Hitherto, says Florus, the Roman people was beautiful, noble, pious, holy, and magnificent." The Jews, first among the eastern nations, employed their friendship to secure their liberty. Facile tunc Romanis de alieno largientibus, Just. xxxvi.

8:2.text: Year of the World 3816, Year before Christ 188.

They heard, etc. What is here set down of the history and character of the ancient Romans, is not an assertion, or affirmation of the sacred writer; but only a relation of what Judas had heard of them.
The Gauls near Narbonne were then tributary to the Romans.

8:3.text: Mines, for which Spain was most famous.
The place, or every place in the world.

8:4.text: Earth, little known to the Jews, like Syphax, etc.

8:5.text: Flaminius subdued the former, and P. Emilius the latter.

8:6.text: Year of the World 3815, Year before Christ 189.
Twenty. Livy mentions only fifty-four. Florus allows that elephants were on both sides of the army, consisting of 300,000 foot. Appian admits but 70,000 men.
Polybius (5.) asserts that Antiochus had 102 elephants against Ptolemy. In this war he might well have 120.

8:7.text: Alive. He was forced to submit to conditions, as hard as if he had been prisoner.
He was present with the ambassabors at Lysimachia, to see the conditions put in execution.
Tribute. He had to pay all the expenses of the war, 15,000 talents of Eubea, 350 to Eumenes, and 127 for wheat. Twenty hostages were required to be exchanged every three years.
Upon. Greek, "the division," (Calmet) or all Europe, and from Halys as far as Mount Taurus. All his elephants at Apamea, and his ships of war, were also ceded.

8:8.text: Indians. So the Ionians and Carians might be styled, from the Indus [River] or Kalbit, which flows through their country. The Midaei dwelt in those regions.
Some think the Ionians and Mysians should be put for the Indians and Medes, (Grotius) as they were given up to Eumenes.
Yet the name of Indian was very vague, and denoted the Ethiopians, Arabs, etc.

8:9.text: Greece, in league with Antiochus, (Grotius) or the Etolians, whom Glabrio conquered, may be meant.

8:13.text: Reigned, like Masinissa, Prusias, Eumenes, Eupator, and Phiolmetor.

8:14.text: Crown, after the expulsion of the Tarquins.

8:15.text: Twenty. These latter were people in office, the two consuls, pretors, and questors, the four ediles, and ten tribunes. After Sylla the number of senators was augmented from 300 to 1000.

8:16.text: To one man. There were two consuls; but one only ruled at one time, each in his day.
Of this we have an example in the battle of Cannae. The first of the consuls wore the ornaments.
It would have been more dreadful to have two than one ruler.

One of them governed at home; the other in the armies, and this latter alone was known to the Jews. They might also be falsely informed that a dictator was chosen every year. This was only done on great emergencies.

No envy, etc. So Judas had heard; and it was so far true, with regard to the ancient Romans: that as yet no envy or jealousy had divided them into such open factions and civil wars, as they afterwards experienced in the time of Marius and Sylla, etc.
Before they seemed all bent on promoting the public good.

8:21.text: Sight. The Romans expected they should soon be at war with Demetrius, and had heard of the exploits of Judas.

8:26.text: Shall they. The Jews and Romans shall pay their own forces.
Grotius explains it, as if the former were to give no aid to the enemies of Rome.

8:28.text: To them. Grotius adds, "to the enemies and to them," etc., which is the sense of the Syriac and Arabic and the most natural.

8:31.text: Jews. He and his predecessors had abused their power. Yet Rupert and Peter of Blois (ep. 146.) disapprove of the Jews having recourse to the Romans: they might flee away. It is not lawful to take arms against the sovereign. God had, however, sufficiently evinced by miracles that the conduct of the Machabees was pleasing to him, 2 Machabees 15:12.
The Jews were not properly subjects, though tributary to foreign kings, whose design was to exterminate them, 1 Machabees 3:39. Self-preservation dictated that they should call in the aid of others.
To die for religion was a great blessing.

But God would not have his people thus entirely destroyed. The obligations of rulers and of the people are reciprocal. All nations have asserted their right to seek redress under oppression. Yet few have been treated so ill as the Jews. Few have acted with equal zeal and justice.

9:1.text: Year of the World 3843, Year before Christ 161.

Time. About a year after Nicanor's death, (chap. 7:50., and 2 Machabees 15:38.; Worthington) or in the same year, the year [of the world] 3843; (Calmet) so that the Romans had not time to send Judas assistance.

Josephus ([Antiquities?] 12:17.) erroneously supposes that Alcimus was dead, and Judas his successor, while Simon was the civil ruler and general. He[Josephus] elsewhere (B.[Antiquities?] 20:8.) asserts that Jonathas was next high priest to Alcimus, who certainly officiated after the death of Judas, ver. 54.
Bacchides had been already in Judea, 1 Machabees 7:8. Now the right wing, composed of the best troops, and usually commanded by the prince when he was present, is entrusted to him.

9:2.text: Galgal....in Arbella, or to Galilee, in the flat country, Araboth, 1 Machabees 5:23.
Masaloth, or Masal, was in the tribe of Aser, Josue xvi.[xxi.?] 30.
St. Jerome speaks of Arbela, nine miles from Legion. See Osee 10:14.

9:4.text: Berea, or Beroth, in Benjamin, not far from Laisa, or Lesen, (Josue 18:25.) where Judas was encamped, (Calmet) with 3000 (Gorion) ; Josephus says 1000; Rufin translates 2000.

9:7.text: Down. The strongest are not free from the first emotions of trouble; but trusting in God, they are assured either of a temporal or eternal victory.

9:10.text: He seems to have thought God required him now to fight, and he was convinced that he could save by means of a few, which excuses him from rashness. The Church adopts his words, (Dom. 3:Oct. Mat.) and St. Ambrose (Of. 1:41.) highly extols his bravery, in "prefering death before slavery and shame."

9:12.text: Legion. So the Vulgate commonly renders Greek "phalanx," (Calmet) a compact body of 8000 men, used by the Macedonians. The Roman legion consisted of 6726, (Veget.) or 6666. The two phalanxes were in each wing supported by cavalry, which endeavoured to surround Judas.

9:15.text: Azotus. Not belonging to the Philistines, but some place unknown, which Josephus styles Asa.

9:18.text: Slain. Gorionides relates that the sight of Judas made Bacchides flee towards Azotus. He lost 15,000, when the rest of the army came upon Judas now fatigued; and as Bacchides returned to the charge, the hero fell upon heaps of the slain. Thus died this great man who saved Israel, ver. 21.

Gloriosiorem triumphis mortem invenit. The most mighty in the sight of men may fall; but the fortitude of Judas, proved by former heroic actions and by success, was now rendered perfect by this most glorious death.

Never was there a more accomplished and virtuous champion. He had all the qualities of a holy priest and a valiant soldier, as well as of a religious prince, always bent on promoting the glory of his people, 1 Machabees 3:3. He was an admirable copy of Jesus Christ, the great original of all the saints. In the most dismal times he was chosen to save religion and the people. Almost alone, and wandering from place to place, he gains the most signal victories, forms a league with Gentiles, purifies the temple, acts both as high priest and as prince of the nation, and at last gives his life for his brethren. Thus Christ by his life and death unites all in one Church, and procures the remission of sin.

9:19.text: Modin. Their remains were seen there long after.
It is wonderful that they should be shewn at Antioch.
But these were the relics of the seven brothers, 2 Machabees 7:1.

9:22.text: Many. Thus only some of our Saviour's actions are recorded, for the same reason, John 21:25.

9:24.text: All. Josephus says, "Many." We find some (Calmet) joining Jonathan, ver. 28.
When the people are without a ruler, they fall, Proverbs 11:14.

9:26.text: Abused them, and "afterwards slew them all."

9:27.text: No prophet since the days of Malachi, (Calmet) or the return from captivity.

9:28.text: Jonathan, or (Haydock) Jonathas, was the third general captain of this family, and succeeded Judas in the pontificate, though Alcimus still usurped the dignity (chap. 9:9.) as in the lifetime of Judas.

9:33.text: Thecua, seven miles south of Bethlehem.
Asphar, or "asphaltite lake" (Gorion) of Sodom; so called on account of the bitumen, or asphalte.

9:34.text: Sabbath, out of contempt, like Nicanor; (2 Machabees xv.) or thinking that the Jews would not then fight.

9:35.text: Lend. Some copies read, "to entrust unto them." They wished not to be embarrassed with useless baggage. It all fell into the hands of Jambri, (Calmet) who slew John.

9:37.text: Madaba. Greek, "Nadabath." Josephus, "Gabatha." It seems, as the sons of Jamri lived at Madaba, the spouse would not be conducted out of that place, (Calmet) unless they might reside sometimes in the country.

9:42.src: St. Thomas Aquinas, [Summa Theologiae] 2:2. q. 108.; Worthington
9:42.text: Revenge. To punish faults with moderation and justice, is a virtue. Children and subjects are sometimes exposed to temporal chastisements, for the faults of their parents or leaders; yet this is for their own advancement in piety, or in punishment for the share they have had in the crime, or to prevent them from imitating it.

9:44.text: Before, when we were in the desert of Thecua, and could decline fighting; (Menochius) or, notwithstanding the sabbath, we must engage, 1 Machabees 2:41.

9:48.text: To them. Jonathas swam to another place on the same side, otherwise there would have been no battle.
But this had taken place, when a thousand were slain; though Bacchides fled from Jonathan, ver. 47, 49.
The Greek and Josephus seem more full: (Calmet) "Swam over, and they (the enemies) did not cross the Jordan after them."
Bacchides did not pursue the Jews.
Protestant marginal note quotes Josephus, ([Antiquities?] 13:1.) who says two thousand were slain.

9:50.text: Thopo, or Taphua. The two last were in Ephraim.

9:53.text: Castle, on the highest part of Sion, ver. 52., and 1 Machabees 1:35.
Custody, without doing them any farther injury.

9:54.text: Year of the World 3844, Year before Christ 160.

Alcimus. Josephus ([Antiquities?] 12:17.) confesses that Judas was high priest. But he mistakes when he says he succeeded Alcimus, who it hence appears survived him a year, (ver. 3, 18.) and was only an usurper. He should therefore be corrected by this book, rather than it should be rejected because it differs from him, or any such authors.
Walls, before the most holy place, (Josephus; Lyranus) or between the priests and people, (Salien; Menochius) or which separated the Jews from the Gentiles, as if to remove all distinction.
It is probable that he removed the wall which went round the inner temple and the altar of holocausts, (Calmet) which Alexander restored.
Prophets. Aggeus and Zacharias had been as it were the architects and directors of the temple.

9:55.text: House. He could not make his will.

9:58.text: Year of the World 3846, Year before Christ 158.

9:61.text: He. Jonathas slew those who had given information.

9:62.text: Bethbessen, in the desert of Jericho, (Menochius) the same with Bethagla. Josephus, "Bethalaga."

9:66.text: Odares, people of the country who adhered to Bacchides.
Increase, or shew himself in the field with a large army, (Calmet) or to penetrate the army of the enemy.

9:69.text: Slew. Evil counsel is prejudicial to the giver.

9:73.text: Machmas. The capital of Sion was still in the hands of the enemy. Jonathas made good use of the alliance with the Romans, and began to lay the foundations of the national independence, and to act like the ancient judges.

10:1.text: Year of the World 3851, Year before Christ 153.
Illustrious. He falsely asserted that he was the son of Epiphanes, being of very mean birth.

10:1.src: Calmet; Usher, the year [of the world] 3851.
10:1.text: Others say that he was illegitimate, and styled Ballas after his mother Bala, though the name in Syriac may denote "a worthless person." In his medals he styles himself Deopator Eúergetes, as if "God were his father," and himself "beneficent."

At any rate the Romans and Egyptians acknowledged him, being glad to find a rival for Demetrius, who was become odious likewise to the Jews and Syrians. Heraclides had been deprived of his government (Calmet) by the king, (chap. 7:8.) and in revenge (Haydock) took Alexander, and Laodicea, daughter of Epiphanes, to Rome, where he gained over many of the senators, who agreed to help them to recover the kingdom. Heraclides then levied forces at Ephesus, and Ptolemais was delivered up to Alexander, whence he wrote to Jonathan. Nothing contributed more than these divisions to secure the liberty of the Jews.

10:3.text: Magnify, "extol," or grant him fresh dignities. Soter wished to gain over Janathas, to prevent his rival from receiving supplies from Egypt.

10:8.text: Fear. The Syrians in the castle would fear, while the Jews would rejoice, and particularly the hostages, 1 Machabees 9:53.

10:11.text: Did. The wall built by Judas had been demolished, 1 Machabees 4:60., and 6:62.

10:18.text: Brother; the style used between kings, (3 Kings 9:13.) governors, (2 Machabees 11:1.) and friends.

10:20.text: Make. The king could not make him high priest, as he was that already: but he acknowledges the title.
Crown: both the badges of kings, or granted by them, ver. 62., and Daniel 5:29.

10:21.text: Vestment, at the feast of tabernacles, the year [of the world] 3852, seven years after the death of Alcimus, and nine since Judas fell. It is not clear from Josephus, ([Antiquities?] 13:5., and 20:7.) whether the people chose him high priest, or they rather pressed him to accept the king's offer.

10:23.text: Prevented, by sending presents. Demetrius had sent a letter and liberated the hostages, whom Jonathas might justly receive without declaring for the king.

10:25.text: Jews. He perhaps wished to set them at variance with Jonathan, to whom alone Alexander had addressed his letter.

10:26.text: Heard. He scruples not to tell a lie, for he had been informed of the contrary, ver. 22.
"He prudently chose rather to appear to find them innocent, than to make them so," (Grotius) by granting them pardon.

10:29.text: Salt, of which there were pits in the country, 1 Machabees 11:35.
Crowns. Antiochus the great had before granted this exemption to the ministers of the temple.
The Jews, after its ruin, used to present such to their patriarchs. But the Roman emperors claimed them.

10:30.text: Cities, or cantons. Greek, "nomes," specified [in] 1 Machabees 11:34.

10:31.text: Free; "an asylum" (Josephus) without any foreign troops, like Delphi.

10:31.src: Vaillant, the year [of the kingdom of the Greeks] 174.

10:32.text: Castle. Yet Jonathas was afterwards forced to besiege it.

10:33.text: Cattle, so that they shall not be forced to work for the public.

10:34.text: Appointed "of lots," Judith, the dedication, etc. These days were privileged, that the people might go to the temple without being molested, and carry what they would free of all taxes.

10:36.text: Let. This is not required, but a proof of confidence.
Great king. He assumes the titles of the Persian monarchs.

10:37.text: Trust, to guard the king, (Josephus) or be in the treasury.
Laws. He revokes the impious edicts of Epiphanes.

10:39.text: Ptolemais. This was not a great present, as it was in the hands of his rival. But he wished the Jews to retake it.

10:41.text: Above. He was not in a condition to force the payment of these arrears, and is liberal of what he does not possess.

10:42.text: Bagoses exacted fifty drachmas for every lamb, before the daily sacrifice could be offered.

10:43.text: Free. Their persons and goods are protected in this asylum. Even the king's debtors are not excluded.

10:46.text: No credit. Thus would all prudent people judge.
Alexander had moreover made the first offers, and was supported by the Romans. The Jews had not to examine his title, which was admitted by many in Syria.

10:48.text: Year of the World 3854, Year before Christ 150.

10:50.text: The left wing proved stronger. But the right, where Demetrius fought in person, was forced to yield; and the king plunging with horse into a bog, was pierced with arrows, fighting bravely. He had reigned twelve years, and fell a victim to the surrounding kings, whom he had not conciliated.

10:51.text: Ptolemee, surnamed Philometor, (Challoner) by whose aid Alexander had obtained the victory.
This Philometor decided in favour of the Jews against the Samaritans, because the succession of priests was preserved among the former.
Our Saviour also judged their cause preferable, John 4:22.

10:61.text: Pestilent. So the impious are styled, Psalm 1:1., etc.

10:64.text: Away. When calumniators see the innocent honoured, they are afraid to persecute them.

10:65.text: Dominion. General of his armies, (Vatable) or in Judea.

10:67.text: Year of the World 3856, Year before Christ 148.

Son. The king had sent his two sons, this Nicator and Sidetes, to Cnidus, that they might be out of danger. The eldest being informed that Alexander had given himself up to debauchery, put himself at the head of an army which Lasthenes of Crete had raised.

10:69.text: Apollonius, who had deserted Alexander, the year [of the world] 3856, though appointed governor of Coelosyria by him.

10:70.text: Mountains. I seem to be fighting with them. Come out, and shew your valour.

10:72.text: Twice. He alludes perhaps to the defeat of Azarias, and to that where Judas was slain.
This captain had also twice thought proper to retreat, 1 Machabees 6:47., and 7:32.

10:77.text: Army. Josephus says eight thousand foot, etc.

10:80.text: Army. Literally, "camp," which the Vulgate puts for an army chiefly of foot. Josephus intimates that Jonathas drew up his forces in the shape "of a tile," or phalanx, and ordered them to face about on every side. (Calmet) the enemy could only throw darts, but could not penetrate.

10:81.text: Fatigued. In the evening, Simon seeing the enemy's cavalry tired, fell upon the infantry.

10:83.text: And. Et qui seems to be mistaken for equi, "the horsemen," as the Greek, Syriac, and Josephus read.
Temple. This is the sense of the Greek.
Vulgate has literally, "their idols." See Judges 16:23.

10:89.text: Buckle, to be worn on the shoulder.
This honour was very extraordinary, 2 Machabees 11:35.
The highest officers of the court of Persia bore this title, 1 Machabees 11:31., Esther 16:11.

11:1.text: Year of the World 3858, Year before Christ 146.
Shore. By this hyperbole, used in Scripture, we are given to understand that the army was greater than could be easily numbered or conceived.
Philometor had assisted Alexander, with a treacherous design of seizing the kingdom when it should be weakened.

11:1.src: Livy lii.; Usher, the year [of the world] 3858.
11:1.text: He had given him his daughter for the same purpose; and now, when Demetrius asserted his claims, he pretended that he would defend his son-in-law, and was thus admitted into all the towns. At Ptolemais he declared his real design, still pretending that Alexander had laid snares for him. While the latter was raising forces in Cilicia, he took Antioch, where he placed two diadems on his head; slew the cruel Ammonius, who had chiefly irritated the people against his master; gave the wife of Alexander to Demetrius; and, in conjunction with him, went to attack Alexander near the river oenoparas. Here he received some mortal wounds; though Alexander was routed, and soon after slain in Arabia.

11:4.text: Way. This was done to irritate him against Jonathas, but he had now something else to do.

11:7.text: Eleutherus, about eighty leagues. Some place this river between Tyre and Sidon, but it ran to the north of Libanus, 1 Machabees 12:24.

11:8.text: Seleucia. Literally, of the cities to Seleucia, on the sea shore," (Haydock) at the mouth of the Orontes, (Calmet) or Belus.

11:9.text: Hath. To what meanness will not ambition stoop! These kings made light of adultery; and Cleopatra was still more dissolute, and married a third.

11:10.text: Kill me. Josephus thinks this was true: but it seems only a pretext.

11:13.text: Antioch. The whole province rebelled on account of the cruelties of Ammonius, who put on women's clothes, but could not escape. Philometor was proclaimed king, yet besought the people to forget the injuries of Soter, and receive his son, as for his part he was content with Egypt.
Perhaps he saw that the time was not come for his annexing Syria to his dominions, though that was his real and primary intention.

11:14.text: Places of Syria, (Calmet) and the parts adjoining to Cilicia, (Haydock) which always continued faithful.

11:15.text: Year of the World 3859, Year before Christ 145.

11:17.text: Zabdiel, "God's dowry," or Diocles, "the glory of God," (Haydock) prince of Arabia.
Only five accompanied Alexander. His generals sought to make their peace with his rival, and slew him.
Exalted. Alas! to become the victim of death in the space of a week!

11:18.text: After. He had been mortally wounded in the battle, and remained insensible four days. When the head of Alexander was brought, he came to himself; but died three days after, while the physicians were trepanning him.
Camp. The Egyptians loudly complained of this ingratitude of Demetrius.
Perhaps he saw through the perfidy of Philometor, who had designed to keep the kingdom.

11:20.text: Castle. Soter's offers had not been accepted, 1 Machabees 10:22. Jonathan thought himself independent of his son.

11:21.text: Nations, by their actions. They wished to be free from the restraint of the law.
When pastors seek to extirpate sin, the wicked represent this to princes as dangerous to the state.

11:23.text: Priests. These would testify that what he said in his defence was true.
Zealous men will not cease to repress wickedness, as God's word is not tied, 2 Timothy 2:9.

11:27.text: Friends. Greek, "he set him at the head of his first friends," (Haydock) treating him with the greatest distinction.
Wise kings will always esteem zealous ministers, knowing that their fidelity to God is an assurance that they will also be faithful to their sovereign.

11:28.text: Governments, (toparchias) or "principalities." These had already been given, and are now enriched with immunities, like the rest of Judea and Samaria.
The latter was not one of the governments, but they were taken from that country and from Galilee. Jonathas redeems all taxes.

11:31.text: Parent. Lasthenes had procured him troops, and was made prime minister. But abusing his power, threw his master into many difficulties.

11:34.text: Apherema. This city is specified in Greek.
Syriac styles it Aphrem: probably a town of Ephraim; (John 11:54.) perhaps Ephra, where Gedeon was born, Judges 6:11.

11:35.text: Of them. He remits only the arrears, (Grotius) or all future claims, which are given up to the priests.
Pans, or "lakes." Some would substitute (Calmet) timas, "dues," but without necessity.
The lake of Sodom furnished salt of a sharper and more digestive nature than the other. (Galien 4:19.) See 1 Machabees 10:29.

11:38.text: Year of the World 3860, Year before Christ 144.
Nations, particularly Crete. This most pernicious counsel was probably given by Lasthenes.
All people dislike foreigners to be trusted more than natural born subjects.

11:39.text: Tryphon, styled Diodotus before he mounted the throne.

11:46.text: Fight. The king attempted to disarm the citizens of Antioch; but they revolted, and would have slain him in his palace, (Diodorus, Valesii.) had not the Jews in the country, and those sent by Jonathas, come to his aid. He did not, however, give them a suitable reward.

Three thousand faithful slew 100,000 infidels; (Worthington) or they were joined by others who were already in the king's army, (Haydock) or in the neighbourhood, (Calmet) as well as by the rest of the foreign troops, though the Jews were the most active and numerous.

11:54.text: He took the title of Theos Epiphanes, "God appearing;" and that of Nicephorus, "the victorious," when he took Antioch.

11:57.text: Four. The three specified, (ver. 34.) and probably Ptolemais, 1 Machabees 10:39.

11:58.text: In gold. Kings alone had or granted this privilege, (Calmet) as well as to wear purple or a chain of gold, (Worthington) or buckle, 1 Machabees 10:89.

11:59.text: Tyre. Eupator had given only as far as Ptolemais, 2 Machabees 13:24. This Antiochus gave the two brothers, the highest marks of esteem, making them governors of Phenicia and of Palestine.

11:60.text: River Jordan. He was then joined by the Syrian forces, and established the power of Antiochus as far as Damascus. Then he proceeded by the sea shore, and took Gaza.

11:62.text: Hand, or made peace by this usual ceremony.

11:63.text: Treacherously is not specified in Greek.
Cades, a strong city in Galilee.

11:67.text: Asor, near lake Semechon, Josue 11:1. Greek, "Nasor," erroneously.

11:70.text: Army. Greek, "captains of the army of the forces."
Only these two captains remained; but there were some soldiers. Josephus says about fifty. Three men would hardly have made a fresh attack, ver. 72.
Yet feats equally incredible have been performed by Samson and by some of the Romans.
The little band of fifty-three entirely defeated the enemy.
The two captains and their men alone stood firm.

11:72.text: Three thousand. Josephus says two thousand were slain.

12:1.text: Served, Syria being so much divided. Hence Jonathas sent to the two most famous republics of the world, and probably to the Jews beyond the Euphrates [River], to the Nabutheans, etc., ver. 2.

12:2.text: Spartans. The city was also called Lacedemon and Theramne.

12:6.text: People. The Jewish state then greatly resembled those of Rome and of Sparta. The high priest and senate ruled, yet not without the participation of the people.
Brethren, proceeding from the same stock. This was the received opinion in both countries, though without foundation, that we can discover.
The proofs might be known to those who were more concerned.
The Spartans, whom Josephus, etc., style Lacedemonians, sprung from Abraham, (ver. 21.) and were long ago in league with the Jews.

12:7.text: Year of the World 3817, Year before Christ 187.
Onias the third, between the years [of the world] 3805 and 3829.
Arius. Greek and Syriac erroneously write, Darius. See ver. 20.

12:9.text: Things; the letters of Arius, as the sacred books testified the same.
This sense appears to be the most natural; or we are not interested in making the present application.

12:11.text: Observances. Greek, Syriac, "supplications." The Vulgate had probably at first, obsecrationibus. The Jews prayed for their allies, etc., to beg for their temporal prosperity, and for their conversion, 1 Machabees 7:33.

12:13.text: Kings. Ever since the time of Onias, wars had been waged against Epiphanes, Eupator, Soter, and Nicator.

12:19.text: Letter. Arius wrote before Onias, though the letter be placed later.
Josephus give it rather in different words. The answer to Jonathan was sent to Simon, the year [of the world] 3861., 1 Machabees 14:22.

12:21.text: Spartans. They had probably some old genealogies.

12:21.src: Haydock; Salien, the year [of the world] 3821.
12:21.text: Yet they might not be true. Few nations can ascertain their origin; and most run into fabulous accounts, if we except the Jews. (Calmet) See ver. 6.

12:23.text: Back. It would hence appear that the Jews wrote first which does not seem to be the case from Josephus, etc.

12:24.text: Demetrius. He resided at Laodicea, feasting, (Diodorus) while his generals attempted to detach Jonathas from Antiochus, but without success. They fled at his approach beyond the river Eleutherus, from which Laodicea was not remote.

12:29.text: Burning. They had been left to conceal the flight.
Grabe supplies, "and they departed," at the end of ver. 28.

12:31.text: Zabadeans. No nation of this name is known; whence most people read "Nabatheans," after Josephus. They had been allies of the Jews, but perhaps took part with Demetrius.

12:33.text: Of it, as it was designing to revolt. Jonathas had possession before, 1 Machabees 10:73.

12:36.text: Mount. Literally, "height," (Haydock) or wall, to prevent the garrison from receiving supplies.

12:37.text: For. Greek, "he approached," which Grabe substitutes instead of "was fallen down."

12:38.text: Adiada, or Addus, (chap. 13:13.) in the plain west of the mountains of Juda.

12:39.text: Tryphon. In a moral sense, Tryphon represents the practice of the devil, who seeks first to deceive pastors. For, as St. Gregory (hom. xxxviii.) teaches, if the pastor's life be corrupt, his doctrine will be contemned.

12:40.text: Bethsan, or Scythopolis, below the lake of Genesareth. Tryphon was not content with governing under the young Antiochus. He was afraid lest Jonathas should oppose his measures, (Calmet) knowing that he was a man of probity, to whom the king had shewn favour.

12:49.text: Plain of Esdrelon, or Mageddo, (Menochius) styled also the vale of Jezrahel, and perhaps Arboth, 1 Machabees 5:23, and 9:2.

12:50.text: Slain. So it was reported, though falsely.

13:1.text: Simon, the fourth high priest and prince of this family, who was judged by his father to be the most prudent, 1 Machabees 2:65. He endeavoured to defend the nation.

13:11.text: Jonathan, his friend, (Josephus) brother of Mathathias, mentioned [in] 1 Machabees 11:70.

13:12.text: Custody: tied to a soldier, like St. Paul, Acts 18:16.

13:13.text: Plain; Sephala, (chap. 12:38.) above Eleutheropolis, by which road Jerusalem was most easily attacked, 1 Machabees 6:31.

13:19.text: Talents. If he had refused, he might have been suspected of avarice or of ambition.
Jonathan would most probably have been slain, and the people would have revolted.

13:20.text: Ador, called Adoraim, (2 Paralipomenon 2:9.) near Marissa; or perhaps Eder, a southern city of Juda, may be meant. Tryphon attempted in vain to cross the mountains.

13:22.text: But. Greek, "and that very night there was very much snow; and he came not on account of the snow; and he rose, and went into," etc.
He gave this out, or intended to go, but went back along the Jordan [River].

13:28.text: Pyramids. In such the kings of Egypt were buried.
Simon enclosed the pyramids with porticoes, supported by pillars all of one piece of marble. Josephus and St. Jerome saw the sepulchres.
One was probably designed by Simon for himself.

13:29.text: Arms, to denote their victories or solicitude for the people's welfare both by sea and land. The Machabees had repaired the port of Joppe, by which they traded with the Mediterranean, 1 Machabees 10:75., and 14:5. Trophies of arms, in reality or in sculpture, were commonly placed on the tombs of great generals, Ezechiel 32:27. (Virgil) (Calmet) -- Simon promoted not vain but true glory, exciting others to imitate the virtuous.
The humble desire honour in God; proud men will be honoured more than God.
How many epitaphs record the ambition and vices of men! Those which praise real virtues extend the glory of God, who is the author of them all.

13:31.text: Slew him. He hired surgeons to cut him, pretending it was for the stone.
Antiochus was only ten years old, the year [of the world] 3861.

13:32.text: Crown. The soldiers declared for him, in hopes of great rewards which he had promised. He assumed the name of Tryphon, "wearing a helmet," or "addicted to pleasure." His medals have always a helmet; and the tyrant styles himself, (Calmet) "king Tryphon, the autocrat," (Haydock) or generalissimo. He sent a golden statue of victory to Rome, but the senate inscribed the name of the young Antiochus upon it. The first conquest of Tryphon was Apamea, where he was born.

13:34.text: Demetrius, who still possessed a great part of Syria. Jonathas adhered to his competitor. But Simon preferred him before Tryphon, on condition that he granted the immunities which Soter had promised, 1 Machabees 10:29.

13:37.text: Greek bainen, which Grotius thinks is derived from bais, a palm branch.

It was probably of gold, (Menochius) like that offered by Alcimus, 2 Machabees 14:4. Some suppose that a collar or garment, adorned with gold and representations of palm branches, may be meant; such as that which was presented by the Romans to Masinissa.

13:38.text: Force. He grants all Simon's requests, which he had offered to do at the beginning of his reign, 1 Machabees 11:37.

13:39.text: Oversight. All sins in some sense proceed from ignorance.

13:41.text: Year of the World 3861, Year before Christ 143.
Israel, the year [of the world] 3861, the year before Christ 143.
Thus, after a hard struggle, the independence of the Jews was acknowledged.

13:43.text: Engines. Greek, elepo leis, denoting engines of a great size, invented by Demetrius at the siege of Rhodes, who was thence styled "the city taker."

Its top was covered with mortar, (Marcellin 23:9.) and the sides with raw hides and iron bars, so that fire or stones made no impression upon it, while people within moved it to batter the walls by means of wheels. When a breach was made, they jumped into the city, ver. 44. See Vitruvius 10:22.

13:47.text: Idols, of a domestic kind. The city had been assigned to Juda, and must be purified, Josue 15:47., and Deuteronomy 7:25.
Mercy is most pleasing to God and men, provided justice and religion be not injured.

13:48.text: Habitation sometimes, to guard against Egypt.

13:50.text: Castle. It had been occupied by the Syrians, from the year [of the world] 3836 to 3861.

13:51.text: Year of the World 3862, Year before Christ 142.

13:52.text: Days; the octave, or the 23rd of the second month, annually.

This was like one of the days appointed, 1 Machabees 10:34. The festival was observed perhaps only during his reign. Josephus takes no notice of it: but informs us (Calmet) that Simon reflecting on the miseries inflicted on the city by this castle, and fearing lest it should fall again into the hands of the enemy, advised the people to level it to the ground; which they did in the space of three years. (Antiquities 13:11.) They did not commence immediately, as Sidetes reclaimed it three years hence.

13:53.text: Fortified. This work employed them six years.

13:54.text: John. Hyrcan, who defended the country, (chap. 16.) (Worthington) and succeeded Simon.
Gazara; perhaps Gadara, (Calmet) which the Jews seized, (Strabo XVI.) or Gaza.

14:1.text: Year of the World 3863, Year before Christ 141.

Two. It is not easy to conceive why Josephus and Grotius place this expedition before the death of young Antiochus, 1 Machabees 13:31, 41. Demetrius was become contemptible, and had a mine to regain his reputation by attacking the Parthians, who had taken many provinces under the prudent conduct of Mithridates. The Greeks were impatient of this yoke. Many []ked[flocked?] to Demetrius, and he gained several victories; but after two years, was perfidiously taken by a general of the Parthians while treating of peace. Mithridates took him for a warning through the places which seemed favourable to him; but afterwards he sent him into Hyrcania, gave him his daughter in marriage, with the badges of royalty, and promises of restoring him to his kingdom.

14:1.src: Usher, the year [of the world] 3863.; Just. xxxvi., and xxxviii.; Calmet
14:1.text: Tryphon. After the misfortune of Demetrius, he abandoned himself to his pleasures; and his adherents put themselves under Cleopatra, the wife of his rival, who resided at Seleucia, upon the Orontes.

14:2.text: Arsaces. This was the common title of the Parthians kings, after the founder of that powerful monarchy.

14:4.text: Days. Few princes have obtained such a character: for how shall they give content to all?
Simon had governed two years in troubles, but henceforward enjoyed peace, till Sidetes disturbed it.

14:5.text: Sea, or to all places to which they went by water. The port was however very indifferent.

14:7.text: Captives, taken from the enemy; (Calmet) or he liberated many Jews.

14:9.text: Land. The judges (Haydock) strove to promote peace.
Robes. Spoils which they had taken; (Calmet) or they were ever ready to defend the country, though peace now prevailed.

14:10.text: Ammunition: provisions and engines of war.

14:12.text: Fig-tree: a picture of perfect peace, 3 Kings 4:25.

14:20.text: Princes. Cleomenes, the last king, died in Egypt, the year [of the world] 3784, and the tyrant Nabis perished long before this time.
Cities. Many depended on Sparta, the metropolis.

14:23.text: Public. Literally, "in the distinct books of the people." Greek, "open for inspection;" apodedeigmenois.
The substance of the letter is here given.

14:24.text: Of Rome is not in Greek or Syriac, and it is allowed that the sequel seems rather to express the gratitude of the Jews to Simon.

When peace was established, and friendship with the Romans and Lacedemonians renewed, the whole nation in gratitude to Simon, who now remained alone of all the sons of Mathathias, confirm him in the office of high priest for ever, or during his life; and decree that his progeny shall enjoy the same, ver. 41, 49.
They should not be deposed, like Onias III., etc.

14:26.text: He. Greek, "He has established: (Syriac, "acted valiantly." Calmet) He, his brethren, and his father's house, and they have routed the," etc., as [in] 1 Machabees 16:2.

14:27.text: Year of the World 3863, Year before Christ 141.
Elul, the last of the civil year, the year [of the world] 3863.

At. Literally, "in Asaramel," (Haydock) "the court of Mello," (Calmet) or "the prince of God," (Serarius) as if it were another Hebrew title of Simon. Protestants, "At Saramel." Marginal note, "or Jerusalem, peradventure,...or the common hall," etc.

14:34.text: But correctionem would agree better with the Greek than correptionem.

14:39.text: Things. Greek joins this with the preceding verse. Some copies have, "all things." He confirmed him in all his rights.

14:41.text: Prophet. This limitation was proper, as the people had chosen this family. The Jews expected the faithful prophet shortly, 1 Machabees 4:46., and Malachias 3:1., and Aggeus 2:7. God was pleased that none should appear for some time before that event.
The high priesthood continued in this family till Herod began to sell it,---soon after which Christ appeared.
The sceptre seemed to belong to Juda. Yet they consent to be governed by Levites, till God signify his will by some prophet.
This consent of the nation, in which Juda was most powerful, shewed that the sceptre was not departed from his thigh.

15:1.text: Year of the World 3864, Year before Christ 140.
Demetrius. Soter, and brother of Nicator, (Calmet) now a captive.

When the latter went beyond the Euphrates [River], he wandered for fear of Tryphon, till he found rest at Rhodes, where he heard of his brother's misfortune, and was invited by his wife to marry her. He therefore took the title of king, when he wrote to Simon form the isle of Rhodes. Soon after he landed (Calmet) at Seleucia, and married Cleopatra, who was indignant that her husband had taken to wife the daughter of Mithridates.
Tryphon's adherents flocked to him: exolescente favore recentis imperii.

15:1.src: Vaillant; Usher, the year [of the world] 3864.; Calmet
15:1.text: Demetrius was styled Sidetes, either because he came from Side, in Pamphylia, or because he was fond of "hunting." Yet his medals have, "Euergetes."
Josephus often uses this title for an independent prince, beneath the dignity of a king.

15:2.text: And to. Greek properly adds, "and Ethnarch, and to," etc.

15:3.text: Pestilent; disturbers of the peace, (Haydock) like Alexander, Antiochus, and Tryphon.

15:5.text: Oblations, such as were presented to the Lord, or remittances. He is very liberal of what he could not get back.
